title: Ratatouille

subs.name: ridge gourd (peerkangai) or bottle gourd (lauki) — same water content and neutral flavour

ingredients.name: red or yellow capsicum, deseeded and cut into strips

subs.name: any neutral cooking oil — the flavour will be lighter but still pleasant
subs.notes: olive oil is preferable for authenticity

subs.name: dried thyme (half the quantity) or a pinch of dried oregano
subs.notes: dried herbs work fine in long-cooked dishes

ingredients.name: fresh basil leaves
visual_checks: stir in at the end for freshness
ingredients.notes: stir in at the end for freshness

subs.name: fresh coriander leaves added at the end — gives a different but harmonious flavour

steps.text: Cut all the vegetables into pieces of roughly the same size so they cook evenly. Sprinkle the brinjal cubes with a pinch of salt, toss and leave in a colander for 10 minutes to draw out bitterness, then pat dry with a cloth.

Heat the olive oil in a wide, heavy kadai or pot over medium flame. Add the onion and garlic with a pinch of salt and sauté, stirring occasionally, until soft and translucent but not browned, about 8 minutes.
Add the capsicum strips and cook for 3 minutes, stirring once or twice, until they begin to soften at the edges.

Add the dried brinjal and zucchini to the pot and stir to coat everything in the oil. Let the vegetables cook together for 5 minutes, turning occasionally, so they pick up a little colour rather than steaming.

Tip in the chopped tomatoes along with the thyme sprigs and bay leaves. Season generously with salt and black pepper and stir to combine. Cover the pot, reduce to a low simmer and cook for 20 minutes, checking once and adding a splash of water if it looks dry.

Remove the lid and cook uncovered for a further 10–12 minutes until most of the liquid has evaporated and the vegetables are completely tender with a jammy, concentrated consistency. Discard the thyme stalks and bay leaves, tear in the basil leaves if using, and check the seasoning. Serve warm, at room temperature, or chilled — ratatouille improves as it sits.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: olive-oil → any neutral cooking oil — the flavour will be lighter but still pleasant (olive oil is preferable for authenticity); zucchini → ridge gourd (peerkangai) or bottle gourd (lauki) — same water content and neutral flavour (cut into similar-sized pieces); basil → fresh coriander leaves added at the end — gives a different but harmonious flavour (add just before serving); thyme → dried thyme (half the quantity) or a pinch of dried oregano (dried herbs work fine in long-cooked dishes)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A wide kadai is ideal — it gives good surface area for the vegetables to brown rather than steam. Olive oil is now widely available in Indian grocery stores (Borges and Del Monte are common brands), but any mild oil works. Zucchini is sold in many metro supermarkets; if unavailable, lauki (bottle gourd) is the closest substitute. The dish works beautifully with rice or chapatti.
